title: Travel Advisories
heroImageAlt: Global travel-advisory map with regional Caution-Weather, Normal-Health and Normal-Security overlays
intro: Entry rules, health requirements and operational disruptions change quickly. Use this page as a practical pre-flight checklist and check the official sources before you travel.

sections.p:
Carry a passport valid for at least SIX MONTHS beyond your travel dates — many countries deny boarding if your passport will expire sooner. Check visa requirements not only for your destination but also for any country you transit through, even if you do not leave the airport, as transit visas are required by some countries.

Official sources we recommend:
• Sri Lanka: dfa.gov.lk (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)
• Your destination: the embassy or government immigration website
• A consolidated rule check: timaticweb.com (the database airlines use for boarding decisions)

Some destinations require yellow fever certificates, polio vaccination evidence or recent health declarations. Health rules can change at short notice — for the most current information, check the destination government’s public-health website and the World Health Organization travel notices before you book and again within 72 hours of departure.

We strongly recommend comprehensive travel insurance that covers cancellation, medical care (including emergency repatriation), baggage loss and operational disruption. The premium is small relative to the cost of a serious medical claim abroad. Insurance can be added to most bookings; ask our team for options.

Severe weather, typhoons in East Asia, fog in Northern Europe and dust storms in the Middle East regularly disrupt schedules. Airlines often issue waivers during major events that let you change dates for free; we monitor and inform affected travellers when this applies.

Air-traffic-control strikes (mostly Europe) and security strikes can ground flights with limited notice. Where possible we suggest alternatives early; otherwise the airline’s passenger-rights obligations apply and we will help you navigate them.

A few habits that travel-safe regulars use:
• Keep digital and paper copies of your passport, visa and booking references, stored separately.
• Share your itinerary with someone you trust.
• Note the local emergency number, your embassy address, and the WhatsApp number of your accommodation.
• Avoid carrying all cash and cards in one place.
• Check the official advisory level from your home country’s foreign office for your destination.

When an event materially affects a route we sell, we publish an advisory here and email affected travellers. Recent advisory categories have included extreme weather, airspace restrictions, and one-off changes to entry rules. Sign up for our updates from the footer to receive these by email.
